one_line_takeaway: A 1445-question, 11-policy jailbreak benchmark red-teaming 11 (M)LLMs shows GPT-4/GPT-4V are far more robust than open-source models (open models hit 40-57% ASR under AutoDAN vs ~0% for GPT-4), while transfer-based visual jailbreaks and gibberish-suffix GCG transfer poorly and semantics-preserving AutoDAN transfers best.
key_finding: GPT-4/GPT-4V are markedly more robust than open-source models, and the gap is large. Under AutoDAN, Vicuna-7B reaches 57.06% ASR and Guanaco-7B 46.90% (Llama-Guard), while GPT-4 defends nearly all attempts (~0.00%). The strongest transfer against GPT-4 came from GCG trained on the Guanaco-7B+Vicuna-7B combination, at only 2.39%. Among open LLMs, Llama2-7B is the most robust (0.14% under GCG) and Vicuna-7B the most vulnerable; notably AutoDAN still elicits >10% harmful responses on Llama2-7B (10.84% with Guanaco-7B surrogate). For vision: FigStep (a black-box typography attack that does not require a surrogate) is the strongest visual method, reaching 35.99% on MiniGPT4-7B and 34.90% on Fuyu, whereas the optimization-based transfer attacks VisualAdv and ImageHijacks transfer poorly (sometimes below the no-attack baseline, e.g., VisualAdv on Fuyu 6.75% vs 8.66% baseline). GPT-4V is essentially immune to all three visual methods (~0.00-0.07%), which the authors attribute to input filtering plus the fact that VisualAdv/ImageHijacks leave the harmful text question intact. On transferability, AutoDAN transfers better than GCG (its suffixes preserve semantics, whereas GCG's are gibberish) and visual-attack transferability is generally limited.
core_contribution: The paper builds a unified jailbreak evaluation benchmark and uses it to red-team proprietary and open-source (M)LLMs under a fair, reproducible protocol. Its own contributions are: (1) a consolidated evaluation dataset of 1445 harmful behaviors/questions spanning 11 safety-policy categories, assembled from existing sources (AdvBench, SafeBench, Qi et al., the GPT-4 technical report, and ToxicChat); (2) a systematic red-teaming study applying a large collection of jailbreak methods (32 collected: 29 textual and 3 visual; the textual set comprises 27 hand-crafted methods = 17 template-based + 10 function-based, plus the two automatic methods GCG and AutoDAN) to 11 models (5 LLMs + 6 MLLMs), including closed-source GPT-4 / GPT-4V; and (3) an empirical analysis of relative robustness and, in particular, the cross-model transferability of these attacks. The individual attack methods (GCG, AutoDAN, FigStep, VisualAdv, ImageHijacks) and the Llama-Guard judge are prior work reused by the authors, not their inventions; the contribution is the benchmark, the standardized evaluation, and the transferability findings.
threat_model: Transfer-attack setting. Open-source models act as white-box surrogates on which the attacker trains an input modification (a text suffix for GCG, a genetically-optimized template for AutoDAN, or an adversarial/typographic image for the visual methods); the resulting modification is then transferred to attack other models, including closed-source GPT-4 / GPT-4V that the attacker cannot optimize against directly. Surrogates used: Guanaco-7B, Llama2-7B, and Vicuna-7B for textual attacks (plus a Guanaco-7B+Vicuna-7B combination), and MiniGPT4-7B for visual attacks. The adversary's goal is to bypass safety alignment and elicit content violating one of the 11 usage policies (e.g., Violence and Hate, Criminal Planning, Illegal Weapons).
limitations: Acknowledged/inherent scope limits: (1) closed models are only probed in a transfer setting (no white-box or adaptive attack against GPT-4/GPT-4V), so the ~0% robustness figures bound transfer resistance, not resistance to model-specific optimization. (2) The authors explicitly note that low ASR on GPT-4/GPT-4V does not imply perfect defense (GCG still hits 2.39%). (3) ASR relies on automated proxies (refusal-word detection and the Llama-Guard classifier) rather than human evaluation, so numbers inherit judge error. (4) Workshop-scale study; the conclusion frames broader coverage as future work: incorporating more jailbreak methods and datasets. (5) A naming caveat in the paper itself: the text states gpt-4-vision-preview is 'referred to as GPT-4' and gpt-4-1106-preview 'referred to as GPT-4V,' which reads as swapped relative to intuition; results here are reported per the table column labels (GPT-4 for textual tables, GPT-4V for visual tables).

datasets_used: Authors' constructed jailbreak benchmark: 1445 harmful behaviors/questions across 11 safety-policy categories (largest categories: Criminal Planning 613, Violence and Hate 254, Sexual Content 242; smallest: Privacy Violation 8, Misinformation 9)
Source datasets aggregated: AdvBench, SafeBench, Qi et al. (2023), GPT-4 technical report behaviors, ToxicChat
Policy taxonomy from Meta Purple LLaMA and OpenAI GPT-4 usage policies
quantitative_results: No-attack baseline (Llama-Guard ASR): Guanaco-7B 32.72%, LLaVAv1.5-7B 17.93%, Vicuna-7B 10.97%, MiniGPT4-7B 9.68%, ChatGLM2-6B 8.93%, Fuyu 8.66%, CogVLM 6.95%, Qwen-VL-Chat 2.39%, GPT-4 0.68%, Llama2-7B 0.07%, GPT-4V 0.00%
AutoDAN (Vicuna-7B surrogate, Llama-Guard): Vicuna-7B 57.06%, Guanaco-7B 46.90%, ChatGLM2-6B 37.83%, Llama2-7B 7.09%, GPT-4 0.00%
AutoDAN (Guanaco-7B surrogate): Llama2-7B 10.84%; AutoDAN vs GPT-4 across surrogates 0.00-0.07%
GCG (Guanaco-7B+Vicuna-7B combo surrogate, Llama-Guard): Guanaco-7B 33.67%, Vicuna-7B 37.36%, ChatGLM2-6B 24.47%, GPT-4 2.39%, Llama2-7B 0.14%
FigStep (Llama-Guard): MiniGPT4-7B 35.99%, Fuyu 34.90%, LLaVAv1.5-7B 25.90%, CogVLM 16.36%, Qwen-VL-Chat 14.52%, GPT-4V 0.07%
VisualAdv transfer (MiniGPT4-7B surrogate, Llama-Guard): MiniGPT4-7B up to 35.99% (uncons), Fuyu 6.00-6.75% (at/below its 8.66% baseline), GPT-4V 0.00-0.05%
ImageHijacks transfer (MiniGPT4-7B surrogate): MiniGPT4-7B up to 52.35% (uncons), LLaVAv1.5-7B ~17%, GPT-4V 0.00%
Hand-crafted textual attacks vs GPT-4 (Llama-Guard): mostly near 0%, but combination 2 reaches 5.06% and prefix injection 2 / style injection json ~1-3.7%; dev mode ranti elicits 26.72% on Llama2-7B
baselines_compared: No-jailbreak baseline (raw harmful question) for every model
Attack methods contrasted: GCG vs AutoDAN (textual, automatic)
Visual methods contrasted: FigStep (black-box typography) vs VisualAdv vs ImageHijacks (optimization-based transfer)
Judge comparison: Llama-Guard (reported in main text) vs refusal-word detection (appendix)
Model comparison: proprietary GPT-4/GPT-4V vs open LLMs (Guanaco-7B, Llama2-7B, Vicuna-7B, ChatGLM2-6B) and open MLLMs (MiniGPT4-7B, LLaVAv1.5-7B, Fuyu, Qwen-VL-Chat, CogVLM)
